name: Super Micro Computer, Inc.

profile.description: Super Micro Computer designs, develops, and sells server and storage solutions based on modular and open-standard architecture, with AI infrastructure representing over 90% of revenue. The company provides liquid and air-cooled AI servers for training and inferencing, with Q3 FY2026 revenue of $10.24B (+123% YoY). The company faces DOJ indictments of co-founder for export control violations, active SEC investigation, and multiple securities class action lawsuits.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Supermicro transformed from a motherboard/subsystem company into a $34B+ AI infrastructure provider, with Q3 FY2026 revenue surging 123% YoY to $10.24B driven by NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPU platform demand [1]. Full year FY2026 revenue guided at $38.9-40.4B, up from $14.9B in FY2024. However, the previous quarter's $12.68B record included revenue pulled forward, and Q3 missed expectations of $12.33B due to customer deployment readiness delays [1]. AI Deployment (2/2): Over 90% of revenue comes from AI server infrastructure, with NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra volume shipments commencing September 2025 and Direct Liquid Cooling (DLC) solutions positioning SMCI as a critical AI data center enabler [2]. Market Share (1/2): A meaningful player in AI server assembly but operates with commodity-like margins — non-GAAP gross margin improved to 10.1% but remains thin, and 63% customer concentration with one hyperscaler creates fragility [1]. Pipeline (1/2): Expanding liquid-cooled AI server capacity and manufacturing across U.S. and international facilities, though component shortages and customer readiness delays create uncertainty [1]. R&D (1/1): Founder CEO Charles Liang's engineering-first culture since 1993 maintains design velocity that rivals Dell and HPE cannot match [2].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): Supermicro's mission focuses on delivering high-performance server technology with 'Green Computing' — functional but generic, interchangeable with any hardware assembler. The Green IT messaging is severely undermined by the pattern of export control violations and governance failures [2][3]. Community (0/2): No evidence of significant charitable programs or community investment beyond standard corporate operations. The company's social impact narrative is nonexistent compared to peers [3]. Retention (0/2): Co-founder Wally Liaw was indicted and resigned from the board in March 2026 for conspiring to divert $2.5B in NVIDIA chips to China [3]. BDO issued an adverse opinion on internal controls despite finding no need for financial restatements [2]. The organizational culture appears compromised by systemic compliance failures spanning two decades. Insider (2/2): Founder CEO Charles Liang has held substantial equity for 30+ years, demonstrating extreme long-term conviction in the company he built; his personal wealth is heavily concentrated in SMCI stock [2].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Supermicro's financial metrics are extraordinarily strong on growth and valuation dimensions despite thin margins. Q3 FY2026 revenue of $10.24B (+123% YoY) with full-year guidance of $38.9-40.4B reflects explosive AI-driven demand [1]. Forward P/E of 10.4x and PEG of 0.91 make it one of the cheapest AI infrastructure plays in the market. ROE of 17.9% exceeds the 15% target. D/E of 121.3% is elevated but manageable for a hardware company with high inventory turnover. The 3-year revenue CAGR of ~62% (FY2022 $5.2B to FY2025 $22.0B) is exceptional. However, net profit margin of 3.7% is the critical weakness — commodity-level margins that leave no margin for error [1]. Q4 FY2026 gross margin guided at 8.2-8.4% with expected improvement from Q3's 10.1% non-GAAP. Liquidity of $1.24B daily turnover ($33.62 x 36.8M vol) easily clears the $1M floor.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Supermicro IS AI infrastructure — over 90% of revenue comes from AI server systems that physically house NVIDIA's Blackwell Ultra GPUs in hyperscale data centers [1][2]. AI does not threaten this business; AI is the business. The company builds the physical rack-scale servers, liquid cooling systems, and networking infrastructure that run AI training and inference workloads. Every expansion of global AI compute capacity requires more Supermicro hardware. However, the commodity nature of server assembly (10.1% gross margins) means switching costs are lower than for chip designers like NVIDIA or Broadcom, limiting the score below the IMMUNE ceiling. AI Opportunity (1/1): The entire $38.9-40.4B FY2026 revenue guidance is essentially AI-driven, with direct hardware sales to hyperscale and enterprise data centers for AI training and inference [1].

infinite_game.summary: Supermicro operates with a distinctly finite mindset characterized by aggressive revenue pursuit, systemic governance failures, and repeated compliance violations spanning two decades. The March 2026 DOJ indictment of co-founder Wally Liaw for conspiring to divert $2.5B in NVIDIA chips to China, following the 2024 EY auditor resignation, 2020 SEC accounting charges, and 2006 Iran export violations, reveals an organization that consistently prioritizes short-term revenue over institutional integrity and legal compliance.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (0/3): Supermicro has no articulated Just Cause beyond server assembly and revenue growth. The 'Green Computing' messaging is marketing, not a lived purpose — it is directly contradicted by a 20-year pattern of sanctions evasion (Iran 2006, Russia via shell companies, China chip smuggling 2026) that prioritizes sales over legal compliance [2][3]. The company exists to build and sell servers at high volume with thin margins. There is no vision of a better future driving strategic decisions; profit maximization takes priority over every other consideration.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): The organizational culture shows severe trust deficits. Co-founder Wally Liaw was federally indicted and resigned from the board in March 2026 for conspiring to divert $2.5B in NVIDIA chips to China [3]. Auditor EY resigned in 2024 citing concerns about 'governance, transparency, and internal controls' [2]. BDO issued an adverse opinion on internal controls. The company engaged a forensic accounting firm in April 2026 for a full-scale internal investigation [3]. Multiple securities class action lawsuits allege the company concealed illegal export revenue. This pattern suggests an environment where compliance concerns are suppressed rather than surfaced.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Supermicro competes primarily on price and speed-to-market in AI server assembly against Dell and HPE, with no evidence of acknowledging rivals' strengths or using competitive pressure for self-improvement. The company's competitive approach is finite — undercutting on price and accepting commodity margins (10.1% gross margin) to win volume [1]. There is no indication of learning from Dell's services model or HPE's enterprise relationships.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The transformation from a $5.2B server components company (FY2022) to a $34B+ AI infrastructure provider (TTM) in three years is undeniably dramatic and represents one of the most rapid revenue transformations in technology history [1][2]. Supermicro's early investment in rack-scale liquid-cooled AI server designs and NVIDIA partnership positioning created the capacity to capture massive AI infrastructure demand. While this pivot was market-driven rather than cause-driven, the scale of reinvention earns full marks on this dimension.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): Leadership has consistently chosen the easy, profitable path over the principled one. The pattern of export control violations across 20 years — Iran (2006 guilty plea), Russia (shell company shipments), China ($2.5B chip smuggling, 2026 indictment) — demonstrates that when faced with choosing between legal compliance and revenue, SMCI leadership chose revenue every time [3]. Promoting a chief compliance officer only after a stock rout and federal charges is reactive crisis management, not courageous leadership. The April 2026 engagement of a forensic accounting firm further confirms governance failures.

investment_thesis: Supermicro at 10.4x forward earnings is the cheapest pure-play AI infrastructure stock in the market, with Q3 FY2026 revenue of $10.24B (+123% YoY), full-year guidance of $38.9-40.4B, and gross margins improving to 10.1% from 6.4% last quarter [1]. The company's rack-scale liquid cooling expertise and NVIDIA partnership make it a critical supplier to hyperscale AI data center buildouts. EPS of $0.84 beat expectations of $0.62, and Q4 guidance of $11-12.5B implies continued growth. If AI capex continues accelerating — with Microsoft, Google, and Meta each guiding $75B+ in annual spend — SMCI sits at the epicenter of the infrastructure boom at a fraction of peer valuations.
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe and multi-layered. Co-founder Wally Liaw was indicted in March 2026 for conspiring to divert $2.5B in NVIDIA chips to China, triggering a 33% single-day stock decline [3]. Multiple securities class action lawsuits allege the company concealed illegal export revenue. A forensic accounting firm was engaged in April 2026 for a full-scale internal investigation [3]. BDO's adverse opinion on internal controls, following EY's unprecedented auditor resignation in 2024, signals systemic governance failure [2]. The stock has declined ~80% from its 2024 peak. Gross margin of 10.1% leaves virtually no cushion for pricing pressure or revenue disruption. Customer concentration of ~63% in one hyperscaler creates existential single-point-of-failure risk. NVIDIA could restrict GPU supply if export control violations continue, which would be catastrophic for the business model [3].
key_catalysts: Q4 FY2026 revenue guidance of $11-12.5B with gross margin improvement to 8.2-8.4%, driven by stabilizing component costs and improving customer/product mix [1]
Resolution of DOJ investigation without corporate-level charges, which would remove the primary governance overhang and potentially restore some of the $16B+ market cap lost since the indictment [3]
Gross margin recovery toward double-digit levels as NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra supply normalizes and SMCI's liquid cooling premium commands better pricing [1]
key_risks: Co-founder indicted for $2.5B chip smuggling to China could trigger NVIDIA supply restrictions, additional regulatory action, or customer defections that would be existential for the business [3]
63% revenue concentration in one hyperscale customer creates catastrophic downside if that relationship deteriorates or the customer diversifies to Dell/HPE [1]

Multiple securities fraud class actions and ongoing forensic accounting investigation could result in financial restatements, SEC enforcement, or management changes that disrupt operations during the critical AI buildout phase [2][3]
